summary: Spolsky examines why WiFi consistently fails at tech conferences, drawing from his own experience at Stack Overflow DevDays where the DHCP server ran out of addresses. He argues the problem is structural: conference organizers are focused on finding any available venue, not evaluating network infrastructure, and venues themselves don't understand the technical requirements. Most venues outsource their internet to unknown vendors who install consumer-grade equipment. Spolsky suggests practical solutions like providing hardwired access points for heavy users and asking specific technical questions about access points, DHCP capacity, and bandwidth before booking. He concludes by directing readers to ServerFault to crowdsource best practices.
key_points: WiFi was never designed to handle thousands of simultaneous connections in a single ballroom, making conference connectivity a fundamentally hard problem
Conference organizers face severe venue constraints and internet quality rarely factors into venue selection when only three options are available
Venues universally claim excellent internet access but often run consumer-grade routers on DSL lines installed by unknown vendors
Practical mitigations exist, such as multiple managed access points, hardwired ethernet for heavy users, and dedicated bandwidth for speakers and press
Organizers should ask specific technical questions about access point count, DHCP capacity, and total bandwidth before booking to avoid surprises
money_quotes: Sometimes I feel like I'm lucky if it works in my apartment.
When you try to ask complicated questions and explain that your conference has a lot of techies, they say, yes, we understand, we have A-number-1 internet access, no problem very good.

Half the time, that vendor installed a DSL line from the local telco and hooked it up to a LinkSys WRT54g they got at Costco, then installed some kind of crappola welcome-screen software just to make it even worse, and then disappeared.
I never assume WiFi is going to work whenever I'm in a room with that many techies.
Internet access is something of a black box.
All the venues tell you they have fantastic, soo-perb A-number-1 internet access.
key_insight: Conference WiFi fails not because the technology is impossible but because internet infrastructure is a black box that conference organizers lack the leverage and expertise to evaluate, and venues lack the incentive to honestly assess.
